glossary.definition: The study is to proceed on the basis of the conjecture that every aspect of learning or any other feature of intelligence can in principle be so precisely described that a machine can be made to simulate it. An attempt will be made to find how to make machines use language, form abstractions and concepts, solve kinds of problems now reserved for humans, and improve themselves...
glossary.sentence: The first definition of artificial intelligence was offered by John McCarthy in 1956.

glossary.definition: It is believed that humans, animals and even plants possess intelligence.
glossary.sentence: To figure out what artificial intelligence is, this concept should be considered in a close connection with our understanding of what is intelligence in general.

glossary.definition: Human intelligence is studied in-depth in many fields of science, such as philosophy, linguistics, neuroscience, etc. It is psychology that has offered a set of theories about the nature of human intelligence and has defined many characteristics of human intelligence. Moreover, this field of science has also developed a set of tests allowing assessment of the level of human intellectual development.
glossary.sentence: Human intelligence is studied in-depth in many fields of science, such as philosophy, linguistics, neuroscience, etc.

glossary.definition: It is mostly related to learning ability of such animals as monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows. However it is even more challenging to measure animal intelligence than human intelligence, because animal diversity is even greater taking into account their cognitive and perceptual capacities, available sensory organs and the ways how they explore the environment in which they live.
glossary.sentence: It is problematic to measure intelligence due to the diversity of people... As a result, the question arises what kind of human intelligence should be adopted as a standard in the development of intelligent computer systems...

glossary.definition: Plant intelligence is not based on a brain and nervous system. At the same time, plants possess many forms of intellectual performance, such as: they learn based on repeated practice; they have memory; they are able to percept a number of aspects (shape, colour, smell and sound) at the same time; they have a “sense of place” and an awareness of the neighbourhood.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge about plant intelligence is even more limited. It is currently known that plant intelligence is not based on a brain and nervous system

glossary.definition: encompass the essence of the different types of intelligence; not be limited to any particular set of senses, environments, goals, and any specific kind of intelligence implementation structures; be based on principles which are fundamental and thus unlikely to alter over time; ideally be formally expressed, objective, and practically realisable as an effective intelligence test.
glossary.sentence: In order to cover the diversity of the existent manifestations of intelligent performance, a general definition of intelligence should

glossary.definition: a standard... adopt[ed] an ideal intelligence called rationality as a standard
glossary.sentence: the categories on the right encompass definitions that adopt an ideal intelligence called rationality as a standard

glossary.definition: Systems that think humanly. It considers human being to be an information processing system. Therefore, research works focus on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in the human mind and in computers.
glossary.sentence: A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly.

glossary.definition: knowledge is represented as logical rules, for example, if we know that “All students have a student ID” and “Alex is a student”, than we can conclude (to infer new knowledge) that “Alex has a student ID”.
glossary.sentence: Systems that think rationally are based on logics

glossary.definition: the development of such intelligent computer systems that can reach human-level performance in all cognitive tasks.
glossary.sentence: The approach of the Turing test underlies systems that act humanly.

glossary.definition: models intellectual behaviour using explicit symbolic representations of problems and their solutions and rules for transforming these symbols.
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence models intellectual behaviour using explicit symbolic representations of problems and their solutions and rules for transforming these symbols.

glossary.definition: models intellectual behaviour based on distributed representations, learning from data and pattern recognition, without relying on explicit rules and symbolic structures.
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence models intellectual behaviour based on distributed representations, learning from data and pattern recognition, without relying on explicit rules and symbolic structures.

glossary.definition: defined by Newell and Simon in 1976. It defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved and is formulated as follows: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action” (Newell and Simon, 1976)
glossary.sentence: physical symbol system hypothesis , defined by Newell and Simon in 1976. It defines how the general intelligent performance of both humans and computers is achieved and is formulated as follows: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action” (Newell and Simon, 1976)

glossary.definition: knowledge encoded as numerical values in the weights of the connections between processing units in connectionist artificial intelligence.
glossary.sentence: knowledge is encoded as numerical values in the weights of the connections, which are changed during the operation of the network.

glossary.definition: simplified models of the human brain consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain, and numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks are simplified models of the human brain consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain, and numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections.

glossary.definition: consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain; operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.
glossary.sentence: symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain; operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.

glossary.definition: allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures. The operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.
glossary.sentence: operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.

glossary.definition: A physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: A physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009)

glossary.definition: available in the relevant problem domain. However, it is not easy to learn and modify them.
glossary.sentence: This knowledge consists mainly of generalisations that are available in the relevant problem domain. However, it is not easy to learn and modify them.

glossary.definition: describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence. The idea of the argument is the following: Imagine a room in which there is a person who receives an input through one slot and produces an output through another slot. Both input and output are represented as a piece of paper with Chinese symbols written on it. The person does not know Chinese and cannot understand what is written. He/she uses an instruction manual written in English that tells the person in the room how to respond to the paper received. Therefore, the only thing the person does is to match the Chinese symbols received with symbols presented in the instruction manual in some sort of formal way – according to their shape, for example. The matching operation allows him/her to identify which symbols to produce as an output. Therefore, the person does not understand the written symbols but is able to provide the right output.
glossary.sentence: John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism. Searle describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence. The idea of the argument is the following. Imagine a room in which there is a person who receives an input through one slot and produces an output through another slot. Both input and output are represented as a piece of paper with Chinese symbols written on it. The person does not know Chinese and cannot understand what is written. He/she uses an instruction manual written in English that tells the person in the room how to respond to the paper received. Therefore, the only thing the person does is to match the Chinese symbols received with symbols presented in the instruction manual in some sort of formal way – according to their shape, for example. The matching operation allows him/her to identify which symbols to produce as an output. Therefore, the person does not understand the written symbols but is able to provide the right output.

glossary.definition: a program designed to play the board game Go that uses tree-based search complemented by artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: tree-based search complements artificial neural networks in the AlphaGo program, which is designed to play the board game Go (Goel, 2021).

glossary.definition: knowledge is not directly represented in the system because it is considered as a distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units, rather than a quantity encoded in physical symbols.
glossary.sentence: knowledge is not directly represented in the system because it is considered as a distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units, rather than a quantity encoded in physical symbols

glossary.definition: attached to the connections between units in artificial neural networks, reflecting the strength of these connections.
glossary.sentence: numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections.

glossary.definition: processing units in artificial neural networks that mimic the neurons of the human brain.
glossary.sentence: consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain

glossary.definition: complements artificial neural networks in the AlphaGo program, which is designed to play the board game Go.
glossary.sentence: tree-based search complements artificial neural networks in the AlphaGo program, which is designed to play the board game Go (Goel, 2021).

glossary.definition: A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action” (Newell and Simon, 1976)
glossary.sentence: “A physical symbol system has the necessary and sufficient means for general intelligent action” (Newell and Simon, 1976)

glossary.definition: explicitly represent[ed] by intelligent computer systems based on the symbolic artificial intelligence paradigm through a set of symbols.
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems based on this paradigm therefore explicitly represent problem domain knowledge (objects and their relations) through a set of symbols

glossary.definition: A graph representation where nodes represent states and edges represent possible transitions between states in the context of search algorithms.
glossary.sentence: Nodes located at the depth limit are considered to be leaf/root nodes (depending on the search direction). Therefore, the algorithm does not consider nodes located below the defined depth limit.

glossary.definition: correspond to discrete states of a problem-solving process; divided into root nodes (initial state), terminal nodes (goal state), and internal nodes (intermediate states)
glossary.sentence: The structural viewpoint includes ... : nodes corresponding to discrete states of a problem-solving process. The nodes are divided into three groups: a state space graph should have at least one root node that corresponds to an initial state of a problem-solving process. ... a state space graph should have at least one terminal (leaf) node , which corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process. ... a state space graph may have internal nodes that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: represent transitions between states or executable actions in a problem-solving process; should be directed to show the changing of states
glossary.sentence: arcs that represent transitions between states or, in other words, executable actions in a problem-solving process. Arcs in the state space graph should be directed to show the changing of states in a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: mathematical representation of a state space graph where N is the set of nodes, A is the set of arcs between nodes, IS is the set of initial states, and GS is the set of goal states
glossary.sentence: From a mathematical point of view , a state space graph is represented by a four-tuple <N, A, IS, GS>, where (modified from (Luger, 2009)) :

glossary.definition: a node in the state space graph that corresponds to an initial state of a problem-solving process and has no ancestors
glossary.sentence: The nodes are divided into three groups: a state space graph should have at least one root node that corresponds to an initial state of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that do not have ancestors;

glossary.definition: a node in the state space graph that corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process and has no descendants
glossary.sentence: The nodes are divided into three groups: ... a state space graph should have at least one terminal (leaf) node , which corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that do not have descendants.

glossary.definition: nodes in the state space graph that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process and have both ancestors and descendants
glossary.sentence: The nodes are divided into three groups: ... a state space graph may have internal nodes that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that have both ancestors and descendants.

glossary.definition: (already defined above) repeated here for clarity: the transition from an initial state to a goal state through actions; involves changing states by performing executable actions in a formal representation like the state space graph
glossary.sentence: But how can we describe a problem-solving process in a formal way? To talk about the need to solve a problem, humans first need to become aware that the problem exists or, in other words, the problem needs to be defined. The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process ), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process ) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states.

glossary.definition: a starting point in the problem-solving process; corresponds to root nodes in the state space graph
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process ), ... From the structural viewpoint, these [root nodes] correspond to an initial state of a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: a desired end point in the problem-solving process; corresponds to terminal or leaf nodes in the state space graph
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine ... a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process ) ... From the structural viewpoint, these [terminal/leaf nodes] correspond to a goal state of a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: the difference between the initial and goal states indicating that a problem exists
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine ... whether there is a discrepancy between these two states (Figure 4). ... in your case, the initial state ... The goal state is ... Since you are still in your apartment, there is a discrepancy between the initial and goal states of the problem-solving process, which means that you have a problem.

glossary.definition: operations taken to transition from one state to another in the problem-solving process; correspond to arcs in the state space graph
glossary.sentence: Once we have defined the problem, we can start thinking about resources and opportunities available to us, which together allow the identification of actions that we can take to solve the problem. ... Arcs in the state space graph represent transitions between states or executable actions in a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: a mathematical or computational model used to represent real-world objects and their behaviors for implementation in intelligent computer systems
glossary.sentence: We need a formal or mathematical representation, also called a formal specification (Figure 3). The formal representation uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.

glossary.definition: elements implemented in computer systems based on formal representations of real-world entities
glossary.sentence: The formal representation uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.

glossary.definition: entities, properties, relations, and functions existing outside computer systems as part of the physical environment or domain being modeled
glossary.sentence: Figure 1 shows that we have different objects, their properties, relations with other objects and functions in the real world. When developing any computer system, including intelligent computer systems, we need to think about: data structures ... data repositories ... algorithms ... All these aspects should be transformed into data structures, data repositories, and algorithms.

glossary.definition: a computer system designed to support humans in completing functions or activities by solving problems; requires transitioning from the real world to computational models
glossary.sentence: At the beginning of the study course, you learnt out that intelligent computer systems are being developed to support humans in the completion of various functions and activities, or, in other words, to cope with different types of problems. However, this is not a trivial task, because to develop such systems we need to make a transition from the real world to the world of intelligent computer systems.

glossary.definition: ways of organizing data about the real world for storage and processing in computer systems
glossary.sentence: When developing any computer system, including intelligent computer systems, we need to think about: data structures or, in other words, a way of structural organization of data about the real world; data repositories ... algorithms ...

glossary.definition: locations such as files or databases where data is stored within computer systems
glossary.sentence: When developing any computer system, including intelligent computer systems, we need to think about: ... data repositories or places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others;

glossary.definition: methods or procedures for processing data to achieve specific goals in computer systems
glossary.sentence: When developing any computer system, including intelligent computer systems, we need to think about: ... algorithms or ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.

glossary.definition: an application domain for intelligent computer systems involving diagnoses, symptoms, treatments, and medical knowledge
glossary.sentence: For example, suppose we develop an intelligent computer system for medical purposes (Figure 2). In that case, we have diagnoses, symptoms, medical remedies, knowledge on physiology and biology, medical procedures, medical tests, and other aspects of the world of medicine.

glossary.definition: (implied from context) a branch of biology dealing with the functions and activities of living organisms that would be part of medical knowledge in a system for medical purposes
glossary.sentence: For example, suppose we develop an intelligent computer system for medical purposes (Figure 2). In that case, we have ... knowledge on physiology and biology, ...

glossary.definition: (implied from context) the study of living organisms relevant to medical applications in intelligent systems
glossary.sentence: For example, suppose we develop an intelligent computer system for medical purposes (Figure 2). In that case, we have ... knowledge on physiology and biology, ...

glossary.term: transition from real world to computational objects
glossary.definition: the process of converting real-world entities into formal representations that can be implemented in computer systems
glossary.sentence: The formal representation uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.

glossary.definition: (implied from context) a mathematical equation involving derivatives, used as an example of formal representation for robot arm movements
glossary.sentence: For example, we can describe the arm movements of a robot using a differential equation and then easily implement this equation into a piece of programming code.

glossary.definition: the cognitive recognition that a current situation differs from a desired one, signaling the need for problem-solving
glossary.sentence: To talk about the need to solve a problem, humans first need to become aware that the problem exists or, in other words, the problem needs to be defined.

glossary.definition: recognition of the difference between initial and goal states signifying a problem requiring resolution
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine ... whether there is a discrepancy between these two states (Figure 4). ... in your case, the initial state ... The goal state ... Since you are still in your apartment, there is a discrepancy between the initial and goal states of the problem-solving process, which means that you have a problem.

glossary.definition: (implied from context) assets or capabilities available to perform actions within the problem-solving process
glossary.sentence: Once we have defined the problem, we can start thinking about resources and opportunities available to us, which together allow the identification of actions that we can take to solve the problem.

glossary.definition: (implied from context) favorable circumstances or possibilities enabling action identification in problem-solving
glossary.sentence: Once we have defined the problem, we can start thinking about resources and opportunities available to us, which together allow the identification of actions that we can take to solve the problem.

glossary.definition: actions identified as possible operations within a problem-solving process that can be represented as transitions in formal models
glossary.sentence: Once we have defined the problem, we can start thinking about ... which together allow the identification of actions that we can take to solve the problem. Arcs in the state space graph represent transitions between states or executable actions in a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: specific example of real-world behavior formalized for computational implementation in robotics
glossary.sentence: For example, we can describe the arm movements of a robot using a differential equation and then easily implement this equation into a piece of programming code.

glossary.definition: sets of instructions written in a programming language that implement formal representations in computer systems
glossary.sentence: For example, we can describe the arm movements of a robot using a differential equation and then easily implement this equation into a piece of programming code.

glossary.definition: (implied from context) a set of instructions written in a programming language for implementing computational models
glossary.sentence: For example, we can describe the arm movements of a robot using a differential equation and then easily implement this equation into a piece of programming code.

glossary.definition: (rephrasing of 'awareness of problem existence') recognition by humans that a discrepancy between states necessitates problem-solving actions
glossary.sentence: To talk about the need to solve a problem, humans first need to become aware that the problem exists or, in other words, the problem needs to be defined.

glossary.definition: (specific example) the problem-solving scenario of moving from home (initial state) to a desired location (goal state)
glossary.sentence: in your case, the initial state ... The goal state is ... Since you are still in your apartment, there is a discrepancy between the initial and goal states of the problem-solving process, which means that you have a problem.

glossary.definition: (implied from context) a situation where current conditions do not meet desired objectives
glossary.sentence: To talk about the need to solve a problem, humans first need to become aware that the problem exists or, in other words, the problem needs to be defined. ... in your case, ... there is a discrepancy ... which means that you have a problem.

glossary.definition: A modification of Depth-First Search that overcomes a drawback related to going along indefinitely deep paths by setting a depth limit for exploring the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search is a modification of Depth-First Search that overcomes a drawback of Depth-First Search related to going along indefinitely deep paths.

glossary.definition: a traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking
glossary.sentence: The algorithm searches the state space graph using a depth-first search (Ertel, 2011)

glossary.definition: A predefined maximum depth value that restricts how deeply the state space graph can be explored by Depth-Limited Search.
glossary.sentence: The depth limit should be provided before the algorithm starts its operation.

glossary.definition: Nodes at the specified depth limit, treated as terminal points; no descendants/ancestors are considered beyond this level.
glossary.sentence: Nodes located at the depth limit are considered to be leaf/root nodes (depending on the search direction).

glossary.definition: A list used in search algorithms to hold unexpanded nodes; new descendants are added to this list during exploration.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm takes the node located on the first position in the OPEN list - the node A - and checks if it is the search goal node.

glossary.definition: A list containing nodes that have been expanded; they are not reconsidered for expansion.
glossary.sentence: The CLOSED list is empty because none of the available nodes has been expanded so far.

glossary.definition: Numerical values indicating the depth level of a node, used to determine if further expansion is allowed.
glossary.sentence: Depth labels allow the algorithm 'to understand' if it is possible to generate descendants/ancestors of the current node or if the current node should be considered a leaf/root node.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that explores nodes level by level, proceeding to deeper levels only after exhausting all nodes at the current level.
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search explores the state space graph in a level-by-level fashion.

glossary.definition: Exploring all nodes at one depth before moving to nodes at the next greater depth.
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search explores the state space graph in a level-by-level fashion.

glossary.definition: The target node that the search algorithm aims to find within the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm checks if it is the search goal node.

glossary.definition: a representation that includes all possible states reachable from the initial state in a game or problem domain
glossary.sentence: It involves the following steps (Luger, 2009): create a complete state space graph;

glossary.definition: a node classification in minimax where the player whose turn it is aims to minimize the heuristic measure
glossary.sentence: label the levels of the graph as MIN and MAX;

glossary.definition: a node classification in minimax where the player whose turn it is aims to maximize the heuristic measure
glossary.sentence: label the levels of the graph as MIN and MAX;

glossary.definition: numerical values assigned to leaf nodes indicating game outcomes: 1 for a win by the maximizer, -1 for a win by the minimizer, and 0 for a draw
glossary.sentence: assign heuristic measures to all leaf nodes according to an analysis of who wins at a specific node;

glossary.definition: the process of passing heuristic values from leaf nodes upwards through the state space graph to determine optimal moves
glossary.sentence: propagate heuristic measures of leaf nodes up to the root node of the state space graph according to the following rules:

glossary.definition: a value assigned to MAX level nodes representing the best move maximizing game outcome which must not decrease
glossary.sentence: An alpha number (α) is assigned to nodes at MAX levels. This value should not decrease, since this would represent a bad move.

glossary.definition: a value assigned to MIN level nodes representing the best move minimizing game outcome which must not increase
glossary.sentence: A beta number (β) is assigned to nodes at MIN levels. It should not increase, since this would represent a bad move.

glossary.definition: a pruning rule where paths below a MIN node are discarded if β ≤ α of its ancestors
glossary.sentence: Unconsidered paths of the state space graph are cut off below a node at a MIN level, if the β value for this node is less than or equal to the value of α (i. e. β≤α) for its direct ancestors (referred to as an alpha-cut-off);

glossary.definition: a pruning rule where paths below a MAX node are discarded if α ≥ β of its ancestors
glossary.sentence: Unconsidered paths of the state space graph are cut off below a node at a MAX level, if the value of α for this node is greater than or equal to the value of β (i. e. α≥β) for its direct ancestors (referred to as a beta-cut-off).

glossary.definition: a temporary heuristic value assigned during alpha-beta pruning when not all descendants have been evaluated yet
glossary.sentence: it is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of >=1

glossary.definition: optimal move sequences leading to a favorable game outcome determined by minimax
glossary.sentence: determine the result of the game and the winning paths.

glossary.definition: a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions (Negnevitsky, 2011).
glossary.sentence: Multilayer Perceptron The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions (Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: a type of neural network where data flows through the network in one direction from input to output, without loops.
glossary.sentence: The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network...

glossary.definition: a layer in a neural network that consists of computing neurons and performs feature detection; its neurons are not directly observable from input/output behavior.
glossary.sentence: It has: an input layer...an output layer...at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons...

glossary.definition: a layer in a neural network that accepts input signals from the environment and sends them to hidden layers; it does not include computing neurons.
glossary.sentence: It has an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment...

glossary.definition: a layer in a neural network that sends numerical output produced by the network to the environment and consists of computing neurons.
glossary.sentence: an output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network...

glossary.definition: a function applied by each processing unit to calculate a weighted sum including bias input, transforming it into an activation value.
glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function.

glossary.definition: the learning algorithm in multilayer perceptrons that uses feed-forwarding and backpropagation of error to update weights across all network layers.
glossary.sentence: The learning in the multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm.

glossary.definition: the phase in neural network processing where inputs are propagated from the input layer to the output layer.
glossary.sentence: The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding.

glossary.definition: the phase in neural network training where error is propagated backwards from the output layer to update weights in all previous layers, including hidden layers.
glossary.sentence: performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer...

glossary.definition: the difference between the predicted output by the neural network and the actual known output label in supervised learning.
glossary.sentence: However, in reality, the output predicted by the network differs from the actual output. It is called an error.

glossary.definition: the process of assigning initial random values to weights in a neural network before training begins.
glossary.sentence: assigning initial random values to weights;

glossary.definition: a type of machine learning task where the dataset contains input data paired with correct output labels, and the model learns to predict outputs from inputs.
glossary.sentence: In a supervised machine learning task, the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels.

glossary.definition: an additional input added at each layer of a neural network to allow shifting the activation function.
glossary.sentence: At every layer, we also add a bias input.

glossary.definition: a collection of data examples used for training a machine learning model; in this context, it is selected from during the learning process.
glossary.sentence: selecting a training example from the training set;

glossary.definition: a condition that stops the training process of a neural network, such as achieving a desired error level or maximum iterations.
glossary.sentence: the training process continues until some termination criterion is achieved;

glossary.definition: neural networks typically incorporating three to four layers as per Negnevitsky (2011).
glossary.sentence: commercial artificial neural networks incorporate three-four layers but experimental ones - five-six layers...

glossary.definition: neural networks that may use five to six layers according to Negnevitsky (2011).
glossary.sentence: commercial artificial neural networks incorporate three-four layers but experimental ones - five-six layers...

glossary.definition: a machine learning task where the output layer has a single processing unit to classify input data into two classes.
glossary.sentence: we want to use the neural network for the binary classification task...a single processing unit in the output layer to classify something in two classes only.

glossary.definition: the sum of inputs multiplied by their respective weights, often including a bias term before applying an activation function.
glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input...

glossary.definition: the process performed by hidden layers in identifying patterns or features from input data.
glossary.sentence: at least one hidden layer that...usually performs the detection of features.

glossary.definition: frames that allow creating multi-level hierarchies of class and sub-class frames
glossary.sentence: As a result, the class frames allow creating multi-level hierarchies of class and sub-class frames.

glossary.definition: It represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain (Figure 6). Typically the instance frames include all slot values and are derived from the class/sub-class frames.
glossary.sentence: An instance frame. It represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain (Figure 6). Typically the instance frames include all slot values and are derived from the class/sub-class frames.

glossary.definition: a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame (Figure 7)
glossary.sentence: A referencing principle is used to relate two frames. This means that a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: It relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame. It allows making hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is located at the highest level and the most specific ones – at the lower levels.
glossary.sentence: Generalization relation . It relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame. It allows making hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is located at the highest level and the most specific ones – at the lower levels.

glossary.definition: It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9). In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole.
glossary.sentence: Aggregation relation . It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9). In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole .

glossary.definition: It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).
glossary.sentence: Association or semantic relation . It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: an essential feature of frame-based expert systems. The generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames. Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame (Figure 11). Moreover, frames support overwriting of the inherited values.
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is an essential feature of frame-based expert systems ( Negnevitsky , 2011). The generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames. Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame (Figure 11). Moreover, frames support overwriting of the inherited values.

glossary.definition: Multiple inheritance when a frame inherits slots and slot values from several other frames simultaneously is allowed if slot titles of parent frames are unique.
glossary.sentence: Multiple inheritance when a frame inherits slots and slot values from several other frames simultaneously is allowed if slot titles of parent frames are unique.

glossary.definition: a slot can have a procedural attachment ( Negnevitsky , 2011). In general, it is possible to add regular procedures and/or demons.
glossary.sentence: A slot can have a procedural attachment ( Negnevitsky , 2011). In general, it is possible to add regular procedures and/or demons.

glossary.definition: a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011). We can use such procedures for different purposes like generating an image of the object, calculating some numerical values, such as the amount of taxes to be paid, acquiring data from external sources, etc.
glossary.sentence: A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011). We can use such procedures for different purposes like generating an image of the object, calculating some numerical values, such as the amount of taxes to be paid, acquiring data from external sources, etc.

glossary.definition: a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system (Jones, 2009; Luger,2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992). A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed (Mallach, 1994). Thus, demons control actions for slot-level manipulations (Jones, 2009).
glossary.sentence: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system ( Jones, 2009; Luger,2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992). A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed (Mallach, 1994). Thus, demons control actions for slot-level manipulations (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it; Figure 12 shows an example of the use of demons in frames. Two demons are defined for the Client insurance card class frame: The demon IF-ADDED will calculate the value of the slot Ensured services when a new client (a frame instance) would be added to the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it; Figure 12 shows an example of the use of demons in frames. Two demons are defined for the Client insurance card class frame: The demon IF-ADDED will calculate the value of the slot Ensured services when a new client (a frame instance) would be added to the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: it is triggered when a slot value changes; Figure 12 shows an example of the use of demons in frames. Two demons are defined for the Client insurance card class frame: The demon IF-ADDED will calculate the value of the slot Ensured services when a new client (a frame instance) would be added to the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes; Figure 12 shows an example of the use of demons in frames. Two demons are defined for the Client insurance card class frame: The demon IF-ADDED will calculate the value of the slot Ensured services when a new client (a frame instance) would be added to the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame; Figure 12 shows an example of the use of demons in frames. Two demons are defined for the Client insurance card class frame: The demon IF-ADDED will calculate the value of the slot Ensured services when a new client (a frame instance) would be added to the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame; Figure 12 shows an example of the use of demons in frames. Two demons are defined for the Client insurance card class frame: The demon IF-ADDED will calculate the value of the slot Ensured services when a new client (a frame instance) would be added to the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: it is triggered when a slot value is deleted; Figure 12 shows an example of the use of demons in frames. Two demons are defined for the Client insurance card class frame: The demon IF-ADDED will calculate the value of the slot Ensured services when a new client (a frame instance) would be added to the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: IF-REMOVED – it is triggered when a slot value is deleted; Figure 12 shows an example of the use of demons in frames. Two demons are defined for the Client insurance card class frame: The demon IF-ADDED will calculate the value of the slot Ensured services when a new client (a frame instance) would be added to the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: The inference engine of frames is based on the following principles: to acquire knowledge about some object/concept, find a corresponding frame and check its slots. If necessary knowledge is available in the object frame, return it; if necessary knowledge is not found, then the inference engine can apply the steps specified below and stop when the necessary knowledge is found: perform inheritance (if it is possible ); check inherited demons IF-NEEDED. If they are, trigger them and output the results; explore the local relations with other frames and try to get knowledge from them. Thus, the inference engine searches for the specified property of the object until its value is obtained ( Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: The inference engine of frames is based on the following principles: to acquire knowledge about some object/concept, find a corresponding frame and check its slots. If necessary knowledge is available in the object frame, return it; if necessary knowledge is not found, then the inference engine can apply the steps specified below and stop when the necessary knowledge is found: perform inheritance (if it is possible ); check inherited demons IF-NEEDED. If they are, trigger them and output the results; explore the local relations with other frames and try to get knowledge from them. Thus, the inference engine searches for the specified property of the object until its value is obtained ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: expert systems that use rules to represent and reason with knowledge, capable of handling incomplete and uncertain knowledge through confidence factors.
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.

glossary.definition: factors added to rules in rule-based expert systems to represent the degree of certainty or belief associated with each rule.
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.

glossary.definition: drawbacks or limitations inherent to rule-based expert systems, such as difficulty in assessing individual rule contribution and computational inefficiency.
glossary.sentence: rule-based expert systems have also disadvantages

glossary.definition: a component of rule-based expert systems that searches the entire knowledge base during each iteration to apply relevant rules.
glossary.sentence: in each iteration, the inference mechanism searches the whole knowledge base.

glossary.definition: processes requiring significant computational resources, such as exhaustive searching of a large rule set in expert systems.
glossary.sentence: It could be a very computational resource-consuming process.

glossary.definition: applications that require responses or processing to occur within a short time frame, often without delay.
glossary.sentence: Expert systems with a large set of rules (over 100 rules) can be slow, and thus can be unsuitable for real-time applications;

glossary.definition: the ability to adapt or update knowledge based on new information or outcomes; rule-based expert systems lack this capability.
glossary.sentence: rule-based expert systems are not able to learn from the experience and understand which rules are already out-of-date.

glossary.definition: individual conditional statements in a rule-based system that dictate actions based on conditions.
glossary.sentence: in the case of the considerable number of production rules, it is difficult to find out the contribution of each specific rule to the problem-solving process;

glossary.definition: a repository of knowledge in expert systems, containing rules and facts used for reasoning.
glossary.sentence: in each iteration, the inference mechanism searches the whole knowledge base.
